column12: Explosive and impactful pass rusher who is coming off a dominant campaign. Quick, explosive, and athletic as well as strong for his size. Breaks through double-team blocks to penetrate the line of scrimmage, immediately changes direction or flattens off the edge and pursues plays from the back side with speed. Forceful, effectively uses his hands to protect himself and plays with outstanding balance as well as body control. Quickly gets back to playing form off the initial hit, stays with the action and slides laterally to make plays out to the flanks. Fast in every direction, easily bends off the edge and gives effort defending the run as well as the rushing the passer. Natural pass rusher with an array of moves to defeat opponents who consistently impacts games. Posted 13.5 sacks and 18 TFLs last season. Smart, disciplined and shows the ability to drop off the line of scrimmage and play in space. Stays with coverage assignments and makes plays when the ball is in the air. Crashes down the line to defend the run. Forceful and holds his ground against blocks by bigger linemen.
column13: Lacks bulk and is easily out-positioned from the action. Marginally effective coming out of a three-point stance versus standing over tackle.
column14: Entering the season graded as a mid-to-late Day 2 pick, Bailey has shot up draft boards and now ranks as a top-five selection after his brilliant campaign in 2025. He's an explosive pass rusher who also defends the run and shows a variety of ability. While he has size limitations, Bailey should be a quick starter on Sundays and immediately produce standing over tackle in a 3-4 alignment.

column12: Explosive pass rusher who consistently penetrates the line of scrimmage and impacts games. Powerful, nasty and attacks assignments. Consistently gets leverage on opponents, easily changes direction and immediately alters his angle of attack. Displays terrific hand technique to protect himself, bends off the edge then flattens and pursues from the back side with speed. Keeps his feet moving and holds his ground against double-team blocks. Gets off the snap with a great first step and easily moves around the field. Fluid when asked to twist or stunt. Does more than pin his ears back and rush up the field, rather he reads plays, remains disciplined and works to defend the run.
column13: Easily controlled by blocks or out-positioned from plays. Gets caught up in the trash moving laterally. Arm size will pose a problem for some teams.
column14: Over the past three seasons, Bain has combined for 31 TFLs and 19.5 sacks while forcing four fumbles. And though his sack totals are not outstanding, he's been a consistent menace for opponents, constantly penetrating the line of scrimmage and forcing quarterbacks to leave the pocket or move their feet. He offers scheme versatility and comes with upside, though Bain needs to add bulk to his frame.

column12: Three-year starter who lined up at both left and right tackle. Athletic, agile and explosive. Quickly gets into blocks, stays square and shows the ability to slide in pass protection. Blocks with proper lean, gets his hands into defenders and anchors at the point. Strong run blocker who turns defenders from the line to open up lanes. Displays blocking vision, quickly gets to the second level and adjusts to hit a moving target. Keeps his feet moving and displays solid range off the edge.
column13: Could do a better job sinking his butt at the line of scrimmage and starting with better knee bend. Solid run blocker but by no means a dominant lineman who drives defenders off the ball.
column14: Fano has been a consistent offensive tackle for Utah, displaying consistent progress in his game, and he comes with upside. He must fill out his frame and get stronger, yet heÂ’s an athletic right tackle who should quickly break into the starting lineup on Sundays.

column12: Athletic right tackle who blocks with consistent fundamentals. Quickly gets off the snap, plays with leverage and displays terrific footwork on the right side. Slides off the edge in pass protection, quickly gets his hands into defenders and keeps his feet moving. Quick to the second level, squares into linebackers and seals them from the action. Patient, stays square and steers opponents from the action. Displays terrific blocking vision, picks up stunts or twists and makes good use of blocking angles.
column13: Doesn't get much movement run blocking or really finish blocks. Shows stiffness blocking in motion.
column14: Mauigoa has been a consistent and effective right tackle at Miami the past two seasons and comes with upside as well as growth potential. He has starting ability as a rookie and will only get better as he physically matures and enters an NFL weight training program

column12: Explosive and athletic pass rusher with a versatile game. Agile, quick and smooth. Fires off the snap with a terrific first step, easily changes direction and flattens off the edge. Displays speed pursuing plays down the line of scrimmage. Possesses a closing burst bearing down on the quarterback. Uses his hands well, plays with leverage and knocks opponents back with a violent punch. Consistently plays with proper knee bend and fluidly moves about the field. Quickly gets outside the box to the sidelines in pursuit while also displaying the ability to flip his hips and drop into coverage. Instinctive, quickly locates the ball handler and works to make plays. Shows a variety of pass-rushing moves. Flashed dominance during Senior Bowl practice.
column13: Lacks great bulk and gets out-positioned from the action. Coming off a streaky, disappointing season where he disappeared for long stretches.
column14: After a sophomore campaign which he totaled 19.5 TFLs and 11 sacks, Parker watched his production cut in half with just 9.5 TFLs and five sacks last season. He's an explosive edge rushing athlete with a variety of skill and a high upside, but Parker must tap into his prior play and improve on the dominance he showed in 2024.

column12: Athletic defensive tackle who was also used as a short-yardage ball carrier for Clemson. Fires off the snap with a terrific first step, quickly penetrates the gaps and easily changes direction. Agile, fluid when asked to twist or stunt and plays with consistent leverage. Effective with his hands, slides off blocks and works to make plays against the run or as a pass rusher. Gets down the line of scrimmage in pursuit and nicely redirects to ball handlers. Explosive and flashes strength in his lower body and the ability to knock blockers off the line of scrimmage.
column13: Not a stout tackler and has ball handlers running through his arms. Gets out-positioned by a single blocker on occasion. Not an elite pass rusher for an athletic lineman.
column14: Woods has flashed ability as a dominant force at the past two seasons. He's an interior defensive lineman that will get looks at defensive end in certain three-man fronts. Though his potential is off the charts, Woods is a long way from being NFL-ready and must really polish his game, improve his playing strength and take everything to the next level.

column12: Fluid and natural pass catcher who is feared by opponents and given a large cushion to work. Fluid releasing off the line of scrimmage and quickly gets in and out of routes. Stays low on exit and positions himself to make the reception. Possesses quick as well as strong hands, displays incredible focus as well as concentration and makes a lot of difficult receptions. Extends his hands to offer the quarterback a target and snatches the ball out of the air away from his frame. Plays heads-up football and comes back into the clearing to make himself an available target. Always on the same page with his quarterback. Possesses sensational eye/hand coordination and a sense of timing and battles with his hands to separate from opponents. Lays out or extends to make the difficult catch and gets vertical and contorts to come away with the ball. Works hard even when he's not involved in the action.
column13: Lacks top-end speed and isnÂ’t a true vertical threat. Has been slowed by injury for more than a year, playing in just nine games last season while missing the College Football Playoff contest the prior year.
column14: Tyson is a natural pass catcher with a smooth, fluid style and reliable hands that catch everything thrown in his direction. He beats opponents with football smarts as much as he does physical skills and would be a great addition to a timing offense.

column12: Skilled cornerback with solid ball skills. Quick flipping his hips in transition off the line, mixes it up with receivers and dives around the field to defend passes. Effective backed off the line of scrimmage, quickly diagnoses plays and tracks the pass in the air then shows good hands for the interception. Sticks with opponents everywhere on the field, has a nice move to the throw and competes. Gets vertical to defend passes or make the interception, effectively times pass defenses and does a nice job covering receivers on crossing patterns. Has an explosive burst to the play out of his plant, wraps up tackling and brings opponents down in the open field.
column13: Occasionally shows hesitation in his game. Has less-than-desirable size and gets outmatched by larger receivers. Must be more consistent pedaling in reverse.
column14: Starting on the Clemson defense the past two seasons, Terrell combined for 21 PBUs while intercepting two passes. The younger brother to Atlanta Falcons cornerback A.J. Terrell, the Clemson junior has a lot of upside in his game, yet he would be best playing in a zone system or backed off the line on Sundays.

column12: Large, athletic offensive tackle whose game has significantly improved the past two years. Sets with a wide base, works to bend his knees and is explosive at the point. Effective with his hands, strong and gets movement run blocking. Large enough to out-position opponents from the action and seals defenders from plays. Quick to the second level, adjusts or redirects blocking in motion and fluidly pulls across the line of scrimmage. Quickly sets up in pass protection, keeps his feet moving and stays square. Makes good use of angles and displays solid footwork off the edge at right tackle.
column13: Must consistently finish blocks. Exploited by speed rushers and needs to polish his pass-protection skills.
column14: Entering the season as a late Day 2 prospect, Iheanachor showed tremendous improvement in his game and was dominant during three days of Senior Bowl practice. He's an athletic right tackle with tremendous upside who could start early in his rookie season.

column12: Athletic left tackle who is incredibly light on his feet. Stays square, displays solid footwork off the edge and uses his hands well. Patient, fundamentally sound and agile. Keeps his feet moving, handles edge rushers and stays square to seal defenders from the action. Keeps his head on a swivel, displays terrific vision and quickly picks up the blitz. Fluid pulling across the line of scrimmage and blocking in motion, quickly gets to the second level and easily redirects or adjusts to hit a moving target.
column13: Must improve his blocking balance. Does not block with a nasty attitude. Must improve his playing strength and rarely finishes blocks.
column14: Lomu was effective as a two-year starter at left tackle for Utah who showed improvement in his game over the course of his career. He's a natural pass-protecting zone blocker with upside, yet heÂ’s a younger prospect who must get stronger and learn to finish blocks.

column12: Large, athletic big man who is coming off a breakout campaign. Fires off the snap with an explosive first step, plays with proper pad level and gets leverage on opponents. Effectively uses his hands and commands double-team blocks yet holds his ground. Fires through the gaps against the run and wraps up tackling. Easily changes direction, moves well laterally and displays surprising speed for a big man. Slides down the line of scrimmage and outside the box to defend the run, wraps up tackling and brings ball handlers down in the open field. Stays with assignments and works hard to make plays. Was dominant during three days of Senior Bowl practice.
column13: Must develop more moves, as he gets tied up by a single blocker too often. Not a natural pass rusher.
column14: After three solid seasons at Central Florida, Hunter watched his game take off last year at Texas Tech. He's a big, dominant interior defensive lineman with the ability to be a game changer, and he offers scheme versatility in a four-man front or even at nose tackle.

column12: Game-impacting and game-controlling receiver who is coming off a career season. Quick-footed into routes, uses his hands to separate from defenders and stays low exiting breaks. Stays with assignments, displays terrific route discipline and plays with balance as well as body control. Tracks the pass in the air, adjusts to the throw and displays a sense of timing as well as outstanding eye/hand coordination. Uses the sidelines well, adjusts to the errant throw on crossing patterns and uses his large frame to shield away defenders and protect the pass. Extends his hands to offer the quarterback a target and consistently makes the reception away from his body. Displays terrific speed and burst for a bigger receiver. Exposes himself to the big hit to come away with the catch, gets vertical in a crowd and takes a pounding yet holds onto the throw. Big, strong and breaks tackles after the catch.
column13: Lacks the top-end gear and vertical speed. Could be a little too wide for his size. Suffered a torn ACL late in November that required surgery to repair.
column14: Bell is a terrific wideout who displayed consistent progress on the field then had a breakout campaign last year, totaling 72 receptions for 970 yards and six TDs before the knee injury. He possesses all the ability to be a No. 1 receiver, but now he must return from knee surgery, get back to full health and continue to improve his game.

column12: Explosive college defensive end with a complete game. Agile, mobile and easily moves around the field. Quick off the snap, immediately changes direction and nicely redirects to the ball handler. Bends off the edge, stays within his pass-rush angle and keeps his feet moving. Displays an explosive first step off the snap, flattens off the edge and make plays in backside pursuit. Works hard, gets into space and chases the action. Remains disciplined with assignments and quickly locates the football. Breaks down well, stands over tackle or comes out of a three-point stance and uses his hands effectively.
column13: Out-positioned from the action by a single blocker. Lacks elite speed. Has an injury history that must be checked out.
column14: Mesidor was highly considered by NFL scouts for three seasons and finally lived up to expectations last year, totaling 60 tackles, 15.5 tackles for loss and 10.5 sacks while forcing four fumbles. He really needs to improve his playing strength, but he has the skill and ability to line up at defensive end in a four-man front and eventually develop into a productive starter.

column12: Hard-working, three-down linebacker with a rounded game. Patient and instinctive but also tough. Remains disciplined with assignments, quickly locates the ball handler and flows laterally to the action. Shows speed laterally to get to the sidelines, slides off blocks and wraps up tackling. Uses his hands to protect himself and plays ferocious football with a nasty attitude. Efficient, takes good angles to the action and easily redirects to ball handlers. Gets depth on pass drops and displays terrific awareness and coverage.
column13: Get out-positioned from the action. Lacks elite pursuit speed.
column14: Golday is not a flashy linebacker, rather heÂ’s steady and productive. Totaling 105 tackles last season, he makes plays with his instincts as much as his physical ability and offers starting potential on Sundays.

column12: Versatile safety who receives a variety of opinions around the league. Sized well, keeps the action in front of him and defeats blocks to get to the ball handler. Displays a burst out of his plant, quick to close and effectively times pass defenses. Terrific run defender who moves fluidly around the field and gets a hand in to knock away passes. Does a terrific job with his coverage assignments in deep center field, tracks the pass in the air and gets vertical to high-point the throw over receivers. Hard-hitting run defender who drives his shoulder pads through opponents. Works incredibly well with cornerbacks.
column13: Inefficient, occasionally makes his first move upfield and gets caught out of position. Struggles in man coverage situations.
column14: Smith is an all-purpose safety who watched his game take off the past two seasons, posting 140 tackles while breaking up 18 passes. He possesses the ability to be used as a true center fielder, yet he can play in zone for a team that wants him to defend the run. While some scouts grade him as a late Day 3 selection, I believe Smith has starting potential and should receive consideration in the third round.

column12: Skilled cornerback who showed progress in his game and is coming off a career season. Physical, mixes it up with opponents and does not back down from a challenge. Quick flipping his hips in transition and stays with receivers all over the field. Sticks to the receiverÂ’s hip out of breaks, tracks the pass in the air and has an explosive burst to the ball. Communicates well with teammates in the secondary. Gives effort against the run and is quick to close. Comes off his assignments to make plays on the football.
column13: Small, struggles against larger receivers and get caught up in blocks. Must be more disciplined pedaling in reverse and is very quick to sidestep downfield.
column14: Abney is a terrific cornerback who was rarely challenged by opponents the past two seasons when he combined for five interceptions and 21 PBUs. He's a smaller defensive back who lacks growth potential and would be best at nickel back, preferably in a zone system facing the action.

column12: Quick, surehanded receiver who is super quick in all his actions. Plays with excellent balance as well as body control and separates from opponents through routes. Lays out to come away with the difficult catch, adjusts to the errant throw and displays a sense of timing. Tracks the pass in the air, makes the reception in stride and gets vertical over opponents for the difficult catch. Fast running laterally, competes to come away with the tough catch and displays skill running with the with the ball in his hands. Possesses soft as well as strong hands.
column13: Possesses a small build and is easily brought down in the open field. Did not present himself as a true vertical threat at Clemson. Coming off an injury-riddled campaign and has an injury history.
column14: After catching 75 passes for 904 yards and 11 TDs in 2024, Williams was limited to just 10 games with a hamstring issue last season and finished with 55 catches for 604 yards. He's a terrific route runner with reliable hands and enough skill to line up as a third receiver on Sundays, though Williams must show durability to have a productive NFL career.

column12: Undersized but active college linebacker who is built more like a large safety. Feisty, aggressive and does not back down from a challenge. Remains disciplined with assignments, shows ability in coverage and flows laterally in pursuit. Fast in all directions, gets outside the box to make plays and quickly locates the ball handler. Explodes upfield, works to get through blocks and makes plays behind the line of scrimmage. Goes sideline to sideline covering a lot of area, gets depth on pass drops and possesses a closing burst as well as recovery speed. Breaks down well, quickly flips his hips in transition and stays with the action. Totals from 2024 included 101 tackles, 15.5 tackles for loss and seven sacks along with four interceptions. Junior numbers last season included 81 tackles, and he was credited with five pass defenses. Had three dominant days of practice at the Senior Bowl, looking like the most complete linebacker on the field.
column13: Indecisive in coverage and lets pass catchers behind him on occasion. Not big, lacks growth potential and struggles taking on blocks.
column14: Louis was an incredibly productive defender for Pittsburgh the past two seasons and has next-level scheme limitations. Depending on your point of view, he's either a very large strong safety or an undersized one-gap linebacker. Given his dominant performance at the Senior Bowl, many will justifiably grade Louis as a potential starter on the inside of a 3-4 alignment.

column12: Explosive and game-changing linebacker who is coming off a tremendous campaign. Tough, disciplined and instinctive. Plays heads-up football, stays with assignments and sells out to make plays. Moves well laterally to get to the sidelines in pursuit, uses his hands to protect himself and covers a lot of area on the field. Fast in every direction, very quick and gets depth on pass drops. Works hard to get off blocks and make tackles against the run. Totals last season included 128 tackles as well as seven forced fumbles.
column13: Undersized and gets out-positioned from the action. May be physically tapped out. Struggled during coverage drills at the Senior Bowl.
column14: Rodriguez is a hard-charging linebacker whose game really took off last season when he was a major force on an outstanding Texas Tech defense. He has size limitations and can only play in zone coverage against the pass, yet at worst, Rodriguez will be a top backup and special-teams performer.

column12: Productive two-year starter who is coming off a terrific senior campaign despite struggling with injuries. Fluid, natural pass catcher who tracks the pass in the air, displays outstanding focus and extends or exposes himself to the big hit in order to come away with the catch. Adjusts to the errant throw, makes the over-the-shoulder reception downfield and contorts to come away with the difficult grab. Possesses eye/hand coordination, uses the sidelines well and consistently extends to make the catch away from his frame. Solid vertical threat who plays faster than his 40 time. Gives effort blocking.
column13: Not a quick route runner and lopes in and out of breaks. Lacks a true second gear.
column14: The younger brother to 2021 No. 3 pick Trey Lance, Bryce is a well-sized receiver prospect with dependable hands and a developing game. He possesses the tools and abilities to develop into a second wideout on Sundays, but he will need time to acclimate to the higher level of competition.

column12: Super-productive tight end with an underrated game. Natural pass catcher who displays terrific eye/hand coordination as well as focus. Sturdy tight end who fights with his hands to separate from defenders, consistently wins out for the contested grab and that takes a pounding yet holds onto the throw. Tracks the ball in the air, gets vertical over defenders and makes the difficult catch in contorted positions. Plays faster than his 40 time and shows ability as a downfield pass catcher. Follows the quarterback across the field and works to make himself an available target. Possesses a sense of timing and knows where he is on the field. Combined for 168 receptions,1,566 receiving yards and 14 touchdowns the last two seasons.
column13: Possesses average blocking strength despite his size. Struggles finishing off opposing defensive backs.
column14: Koziol is an outstanding pass-catching tight end with terrific size and upside potential. HeÂ’ll be underdrafted, yet he offers starting potential once he improves his blocking.

column12: Underrated running back prospect with a complete game and explosive speed. Solid open-field ball carrier who runs with lean and has a burst. Multi-cut running back who is quick-footed, follows blocks everywhere on the field and makes defenders miss. Quick, slippery and runs with authority. Hits the hole hard, runs aggressively on the inside and keeps his feet moving to break tackles. Displays soft hands as a pass catcher, tracks the ball in the air and gets vertical or contorts to make the difficult catch. Remains disciplined with all his assignments. Displays terrific contact balance and quickly gets back to running form off the initial hit.
column13: Not a big-bodied ball carrier who moves the pile. Rushed for over 1,000 yards just one time in college.
column14: Claiborne is a well-rounded ball carrier with terrific speed and skill. His ability to create yardage as well as catch the ball out of the backfield, coupled with his effort running between the tackles, sets him up perfectly to be a situational runner or third-down back on Sundays. I expect Claiborne to be selected in the draft much earlier than people are presently predicting.

column12: Nice-sized, run-defending safety who is best playing downhill. Patient, keeps the action in front of him and quickly locates the ball handler. Works his hands to get off blocks, quickly locates the ball handler and sells out to make the tackle. Possesses an aggressive style, keeps his head on a swivel and remains alert. Outstanding run defender who has combined for 207 tackles the past three seasons.
column13: Does not show great range or the ability to quickly get to the sidelines in coverage. Lacks long speed as well as a great closing burst.
column14: McNeil-Warren possesses the size as well as skill to line up in a zone system or as a traditional strong safety. He has limitations in man coverage, but he has proven himself to be very effective facing the action.

column12: Slightly undersized yet super-active linebacker who is coming off a career campaign. Breaks down well, flows laterally to the action and smoothly flips his hips dropping into coverage. Gets depth on pass drops, easily changes direction and covers a lot of area on the field. Patient, instinctive and remains disciplined with assignments. Fast in every direction, displays speed in pursuit and has an explosive closing burst. Fires upfield defending the run, uses his hands to protect himself and plays hard. One of the better cover linebackers during three days of Senior Bowl practice.
column13: Undersized and gets caught up in the trash and easily blocked from the action. Shows no force on the blitz.
column14: After being graded as a late-round pick entering the season, Elarms-Orr totaled 130 tackles and 11 TFLs last season, which helped elevate his draft stock. Questions about his ability in coverage were answered during the Senior Bowl, where he easily defended tight ends and running backs. Elarms-Orr may be physically tapped out, yet his game is on an upswing, and he offers starting potential in a variety of systems.

column12: Fundamentally sound college right tackle who projects to guard or center. Patient, stays square and effectively out-positions defenders from the action. Blocks with a nasty attitude, gets after assignments and works to finish off opponents. Displays range off the edge, effectively uses angles and blocks with leverage. Patient in pass protection, keeps his head on a swivel and works well with linemates. Shows the ability to adjust or redirect on twists or stunts and keeps his feet and hands active.
column13: Must get stronger and do a better job finishing blocks. Sometimes late with his hands.
column14: Parker is a mobile lineman who would be best in a zone-blocking system, but he must improve his playing strength and ultimately learn a new position on Sundays.

column12: Powerful, wide-bodied defensive lineman who is suited to play nose tackle. Plays with great pad level, consistently gets leverage on blockers and easily holds his ground. Fires off the snap with a terrific first step, works his hands throughout the action and attracts double and sometimes triple-team blocks in the middle of the line. Plays with a nasty attitude, goes hard after ball carriers and moves well for a big lineman. Tough to stop, powerful in his lower body and knocks blockers off the line of scrimmage.
column13: Must develop additional moves to get off blocks. Easily tied up at the point. Not much of a playmaker and has totaled one sack and seven TFLs the past three years.
column14: Orange is a rough-and-tumble defensive lineman who does the little things well and plays to his size. More of a gap occupier, he's a zero-technique lineman who can be used at nose tackle or over the center.

column12: Athletic tight end prospect who impacts games as a downfield pass catcher. Moves well around the field, plays to his 40 time and gets down the flanks for big plays. Tracks the pass in the air and battles opponents to separate. Gets vertical and high-points the throw over defenders. Adjusts to the errant throw, catches the ball with his hands away from his frame and comes away with a lot of difficult receptions. Solid route runner who stays low exiting breaks and uses his large frame to shield away opponents. Flashes the ability to snatch the ball out of the air with strong hands.
column13: Doesn't seem to play with a sense of urgency. Does not work to finish blocks. Seems to give up on plays and sometimes loses out on receptions when he should not.
column14: Trigg has flashed dominance the past two years and has the ability to be a productive move tight end on Sundays. He comes with tremendous upside, but he must play to his ability on every single down if he wants a long career in the NFL.

column12: First-year starter who is coming off a terrific campaign. Possesses next-level size and is also athletic. Patient in the pocket, remains poised under the rush and quickly identifies the open wideout. Possesses a live arm, puts speed on all his throws and drives deep passes downfield to allow receivers to run to the ball. Goes through receiver progressions and consistently takes the safe underneath outlet when nothing else is available. Stands in against the rush to get the throw away, spreads the ball around and uses all his targets. Keeps his eyes downfield even when rolling outside the pocket.
column13: Possesses a small body of work and was just a one-year starter. Delivers the ball with a slow, funky release. While he flashes accuracy, he must improve his overall pass placement.
column14: Payton did a terrific job taking over the starting duties at North Dakota State until being injured during the team's playoff loss against Illinois State. Finishing the year with 16 touchdown passes to only four interceptions as well as 2,719 yards, he proved to have the physical skills to play on Sundays, but he is still an unfinished product and will need time before he's NFL-ready.

column12: Nice-sized cornerback who effectively returned from injury to have a career year. Patient, quickly locates the ball handler and fires upfield to defend the run. Quick-footed in reverse and smooth flipping his hips in transition. Displays a closing burst as well as recovery speed. Battles receivers throughout the route, works to track the pass in the air and has an explosive burst to the ball. Quickly picks up assignments in coverage, effectively covers crossing patterns and nicely times pass defenses.
column13: Gets a bit tall tackling at times. Knee injury that limited him to four games in 2024 must be checked out.
column14: Stukes has been a force for Arizona when fully healthy, defending 10 passes as a junior then intercepting four throws last year. He possesses tremendous upside and the ability to develop into a full-time starting cornerback if properly coached.

column12: Intelligent and competitive defensive back who was a productive four-year starter at TCU. Quickly diagnoses plays, displays a sense of timing in coverage and makes a lot of plays on the football. Communicates well with teammates in the secondary, shows outstanding recognition and comes off assignments to make the tackle. Keeps the action in front of him and displays a straight line burst of speed and a sense of timing in coverage. Played extremely well during Senior Bowl practices and was the best defensive back on the field.
column13: Deep speed may be a concern. Labors running downfield with opponents. Slow shedding blocks.
column14: Clark was a terrific college safety who intercepted 15 passes and defended 35 during his four years at TCU. He was also a terrific run defender, accumulating 123 tackles the past two years. He wonÂ’t wow anyone with his testing numbers and may struggle lining up as a true center fielder, yet Clark has starting potential at the next level in a zone or two-deep system.

column12: Two-year starter who began his college career at Middle Tennessee. Tough, hard-working blocker who gets the most from his ability. Possesses great field vision, always looks for someone to hit and plays with a nasty attitude. Sets with a wide base, bends his knees and blocks with terrific leverage. Strong, controls defenders at the point of attack and moves them off the line to open running lanes. Patient in pass protection, stays square and easily anchors at the point.
column13: Shows some ability blocking in motion but isnÂ’t quick or fluid pulling across the line of scrimmage or effective on the second level. Doesn't have the size you want in a pure power blocker or the athleticism necessary for a zone scheme.
column14: Rutledge was a terrific college lineman who showed consistency in his game. He won't wow teams with great testing numbers, yet heÂ’s a Day 3 prospect with the toughness and intellect to be a starter on Sundays.

column12: Big, strong-armed pocket passer who played incredibly well during the College Football Playoff. Patient in the pocket, steps up to avoid the rush and throws a catchable deep pass. Puts long throws in front of receivers and lets them run to the ball. Possesses the arm strength to get the ball downfield and puts speed on all his throws. Sells ball fakes, quickly locates the open wideout and is effective when he goes through progressions. Stays with the action, works to make positive plays and takes the safe underneath outlet when nothing else is available. Nicely sets up screen throws, remains poised under the rush and uses an effective double-pump to allow receivers to come free. Possesses a quick release and throws with an over-the-top delivery.
column13: Streaky. Has thrown 23 interceptions the past two seasons. Will stare down the primary target on occasion. Not consistently on the same page as receivers. Has a lot of passes knocked away or dropped by targets.
column14: Beck was a highly rated signal caller who, for the most part, failed to live up to expectations until his incredible run through the College Football Playoff. He possesses the arm strength and the ability to make all the throws, yet he will have to stay away from bad passes, interceptions and inconsistent play to be a productive starting quarterback on Sundays.

column12: Large gap-occupying lineman with potential at nose tackle. Possesses outstanding size and starts with excellent knee bend. Powerful and explosive at the point. Effective with his hands and keeps them active throughout the action. Bull-rushes opponents off the line, stands up blockers and make plays against the run or knocks away passes. Attracts double-team blocks yet chases hard and works to make plays. Dominated opponents in one-on-one drills at practices for both the Senior and Shrine Bowls.
column13: Inconsistent getting off the snap. Best in a box and doesnÂ’t show ability in pursuit. Gets locked up in blocks once engaged with opponents.
column14: Jackson is a powerful space eater who also shows ability defending the run and accumulated 77 tackles the past two seasons. He offers possibilities as a nose or zero-technique tackle in a three-man line and is a defender who does the dirty work on the inside.

column12: Undersized college defensive end who projects as a pass-rushing linebacker at the next level. Agile, breaks down well and effectively moves in any direction. Athletic and plays with consistent knee bend. Bends the edge with speed and gets a lot of momentum going upfield. Uses his hands well, displays a variety of moves and shows a nice shoulder dip that allows him to get underneath blockers. Keeps his feet moving, fluidly pursues laterally against the run and displays good footwork in reverse dropping into coverage.
column13: Lacks bulk, struggles to get off blocks and gets out-positioned from the action rather easily. Played at three different programs, Auburn, USC and Georgia Tech, before arriving at Texas Tech. Possesses average size/speed numbers.
column14: Height was an overall defender for Texas Tech last season who made a lot of big plays when needed. He lacks the upside of many of his Red Raiders teammates, but he should be a solid complementary situational pass rusher who primarily stands over tackle.

column12: Athletic pass-blocking left tackle who fires off the snap, displays terrific blocking vision and keeps his head on a swivel. Fundamentally sound, quickly recognizes assignments and blocks with excellent knee bend as well as leverage. Patient in pass protection, correctly places his hands into defenders and steers them from the action. Displays a quick kick-step off the snap, shows solid footwork off the edge and does an excellent job with angles. Explosive as a run blocker and gives effort.
column13: Struggled with injury in a disappointing final season. Gets knocked around by bigger opponents. Must improve his overall playing strength.
column14: Bowry was graded as a potential Day 2 selection entering the season, yet he never lived up to expectations for a variety of reasons and now projects as a Day 3 pick. He possesses a terrific amount of upside at left tackle, but he must get stronger and improve from his terrific sophomore season.

column12: Auburn transfer who is coming off a career season at Miami where he was a jack of all trades. Tough, instinctive and does a great job diagnosing plays. Fires up the field to defend the run, wraps up tackling and brings ball handlers down in the open field. Forceful on the blitz. Displays great anticipation in coverage, tracks the pass in the air and has an outstanding move to the throw. Shows good route recognition in zone and possesses a terrific closing burst to the action as well as good hands for the interception. Flies around the field and sacrifices his body to make the play. Finished with 61 tackles, 14 TFLs and five sacks last season while also intercepting two passes and breaking up five more.
column13: Used almost solely in a zone system facing the action and was rarely asked to play man coverage. Comes with average size/speed numbers.
column14: Scott was an indomitable force on the Hurricanes defense and was responsible for many of their big plays throughout the season and during the College Football Playoff. He has a versatile game and looks as though heÂ’d be a terrific dime back in zone coverage who can be used at both cornerback or safety.

column12: Athletic linebacker with a complete game who failed to meet expectations last season. Tough, disciplined and plays heads-up football. Stays with assignments, shows quickness in his all-around game and easily changes direction. Does not bite on ball fakes, shows speed in pursuit and moves fluidly in any direction. Quick flipping his hips in coverage to transition downfield, gets depth on pass drops and immediately picks up assignments. Breaks down well and gives effort defending the run. Wraps up tackling and brings ball handlers down in the open field. Occasionally lined up at tight end as a senior.
column13: Not forceful on the blitz. Occasionally takes himself out of the action. Struggles to get off blocks and gets consistently washed from the action despite his size.
column14: Barton possesses the size, speed and athleticism to start in a variety of defensive schemes on Sundays, yet he never elevated his game after a terrific junior season. He shows flashes of being a three-down defender, and if heÂ’s able to get back to where he was in 2024 then build upon it, Barton will be a Day 3 steal.

column12: Two-year starter at center who displayed consistent improvement in his game. Shows excellent vision and awareness and is fundamentally sound. Bends his knees, keeps his feet moving in pass protection and stays square to seal defenders from the action. Quick to the second level and knocks linebackers from plays. Effective with the shotgun snap. Reads defenses, picks up the blitz and effectively gets his hands into defenders to knock them from their angles of attack. Relatively effective when asked to kick out or pull across the line of scrimmage and block in motion.
column13: Must improve the strength of his base. Struggles to adjust on occasion.
column14: Hecht is a mobile blocker on the pivot who possesses solid size as well as growth potential. He offers possibilities as a zone-blocking lineman and will only improve as he physically matures and adds strength to his game.

column12: Big-bodied possession receiver with long arms and big hands. Displays good short-area quickness, battles defenders and uses his hands as well as strength to separate. Tracks the pass in the air, gets vertical and uses his size as an advantage to out-muscle defenders for the reception. Comes back to the ball out of routes, extends his hands and offers the quarterback a target. Effectively makes the reception away from his frame. Competes. Effective blocking downfield.
column13: Gathers himself in and out of breaks, which slows his route running. One-speed receiver who cannot stretch the defense. Unnecessarily lets the pass get inside him on occasion.
column14: Cameron is a terrific story who walked onto Baylor then showed consistent improvement the past three seasons. A terrific red-zone threat, he combined for 19 touchdowns the past two years. He's a classic possession wideout who consistently wins out for the contested throw and would be best in the short or intermediate passing game.

column12: Smart, poised quarterback who has thrown a lot of touchdowns the past two seasons. Patient in the pocket, senses the rush and steps up to avoid defenders. Reads defenses, scans the field and does not make adverse decisions under the rush. Spreads the ball around using all his targets, displays terrific speed as well as zip on short and intermediate passes and does make receivers wait for the ball to arrive. Displays a sense of timing and flashes brilliant accuracy as well as the ability to put throws where only his receivers can make the reception. Goes to the safe underneath outlet when nothing else is available.
column13: Not an elusive quarterback or a threat to pick up yardage with his legs. Lacks a big-time arm, tries to put extra speed on passes and throws high of the mark when he presses.
column14: Robertson was productive on the college level the past two seasons, combining for 6,652 yards passing and 59 TDs. His stats benefited from a lot of short receiver screens, but Robertson has poise and brain to be a No. 2 quarterback on Sundays.

column12: Nice-sized right tackle prospect who is best in a small area. Sets with a wide base, quickly sets up in pass protection and immediately gets his hands into opponents. Keeps his head on a swivel and works well with linemates. Big-bodied blocker who blocks down on defenders or smothers them from the action altogether. Stays square and seals defenders from the action, keeps his feet moving and makes good use of angles.
column13: Stiff in pass protection, marginally effective in motion and does not play like a top athlete.
column14: Miller is a hard-working lineman with both size as well as growth potential who is coming off a disappointing season. He possesses an upside and is worth considering on day three of the draft, though Miller may never be anything other than an occasional starter on Sundays.

column12: Small but skilled cornerback who offers potential in a variety of schemes. Quick flipping his hips in transition, smooth moving in reverse and battles receivers throughout the route. Gets his head back around to track the pass in the air, possesses an explosive closing burst and works hard to defend passes. Fires up the field to defend screen throws and gives effort against the run.
column13: Gives up inside leverage to opponents and struggles covering crossing patterns. A bit slow out of his transition. Short, which results in mismatches.
column14: Rivers was a productive two-year starter for Duke and projects as a starting dime back on Sundays while offering value on special teams.

column12: Athletic pass-catching tight end who flashes next-level ability. Quickly gets into routes, stays low exiting breaks and follows the quarterback across the field to make himself an available target. Displays good route discipline, consistently finds the open seam in the defense and uses his hands to separate from defenders. Displays eye/hand coordination, adjusts to the errant throw and extends to make the catch away from his frame. Bends his knees as a blocker, squares into defenders and seals opponents from the action.
column13: Double-catches too many throws and does not cleanly snatch the ball from the air. Must improve his route-running balance. Does not finish off opponents blocking and displays average playing strength.
column14: Joly was graded as a potential Day 2 choice coming into the season and has been productive the past two years, totaling a combined 92 receptions for 1,150 yards and 11 TDs. While he shows flashes of next-level ability, he really needs to polish his game and prove he's a natural hands catcher. I expect Joly to be selected in the middle of Day 3, and though he possesses upside, he must take his game to the next level to see substantial playing time on Sundays.

column12: Productive pass catcher who displayed steady progression in his game the past three seasons. Smooth, fluid and very natural at the position. Tracks the pass in the air, displays a sense of timing and is effective running after the catch. Displays excellent route discipline, possesses eye/hand coordination and plays with excellent awareness. Follows the quarterback across the field and finds the open spot in the defense to make himself an available target. Consistently extends to make the reception away from his frame and battles opponents. Effective underneath receiver who does a terrific job making the catch on crossing patterns.
column13: Plays to one speed and lacks the deep burst. Possesses a smaller build.
column14: Bond was a productive receiver at Boston College the past three years and finished with a career-best 80 receptions for 993 yards as a senior. He'll be a solid Day 3 pick and a nice addition to an NFL roster as a fourth or fifth receiver.

column12: Instinctive and hard-working linebacker with a complete game. Effectively communicates with teammates, makes the defensive calls and quickly locates the ball handler. Fluid moving in any direction, displays lateral speed to get to the sidelines and takes good angles to the play. Stays with coverage assignments, gets depth on pass drops and possesses solid ball skills. Efficient, quickly collapses outside-in to defend the run and makes plays in space. Does not bite on ball fakes, chases the action hard and gets the most from his ability.
column13: Possesses a thin build and struggles handling blocks. Lacks elite linebacker speed. Struggled with injury last season and watched his play drop off.
column14: Woodaz was a full-time starter at Clemson the past two seasons when he totaled 153 tackles and 17 TFLs while breaking up eight passes. He possesses a complete game and offers potential as a three-down linebacker in a variety of schemes.

column12: Athletic and mobile edge rusher who is effective out of a three-point stance and standing over tackle. Explodes off the snap out of a three-point stance, plays with great pad level and gets leverage on opponents. Displays a quick inside move, immediately alters his angle of attack and nicely redirects to ball handlers. Bends off the edge, shows speed from the back side and has a closing burst. Effective out of a three-point stance, uses his hands well and makes plays behind the line of scrimmage and outside the box. Keeps his feet moving and plays with good lean. Relatively effective in space, gets depth on pass drops and covers a lot of area on the field.
column13: Easily controlled at the point by a single blocker or washed from the action and knocked from his angle of attack. Does not have great pass-rushing numbers and totaled just 10.5 sacks and 18 TFLs the past three years.
column14: Fano is a mobile and athletic pass rusher with upside, but he must get bigger as well as stronger to have a chance of seeing the field on Sundays.

column12: Underrated receiver with next-level size, speed and pass-catching ability. Comes back to the football and extends his hands to make the reception away from his frame. Stays low exiting breaks, fights with his hands to separate from opponents and offers the quarterback a target. Lays out or extends and exposes himself for the difficult catch, adjusts to the errant throw and displays a sense of timing on receptions. Does a terrific job tracking the deep pass and making the reception in stride. Gives effort with blocking assignments. Performed well during the American Bowl, displaying himself as a legitimate downfield threat.
column13: Possesses a thin build and struggles coming away with the contested grab. Does not show a second gear on the field despite his 40 time.
column14: Smith rarely saw playing time at North Carolina State before transferring to East Carolina, then broke out last season with 64 receptions for 1,053 yards and 17 TDs. He needs to improve his playing strength, yet he possesses all the skills necessary to be a fifth receiver on Sundays, and at the very least he should find his way onto practice squad next fall.

column12: Tall, thin receiver with reliable hands. Comes back to the quarterback to make himself an available target, tracks the pass in the air and consistently extends to make the reception away from his frame. Dependable hands catcher who snatches the ball out of the air and possesses eye/hand coordination. Outstanding open-field runner who is quick and elusive with the ball in his hands and incredibly effective on reverses. Nicely times receptions and high-points the ball over defenders.
column13: Possesses a thin build and struggles in battles. Loses focus as well as concentration. Does not get results blocking.
column14: Virgil was a productive receiver the past two seasons, first for Miami of Ohio then last year at Texas Tech. He possesses enough ability to line up as a fourth wideout in the NFL and comes with return potential.

column12: Productive three-year starter with reliable hands and a solid game. Natural pass catcher who is intelligent and instinctive. Quickly releases off the line of scrimmage into pass routes and stays low exiting breaks. Settles into the open spot of the defense and comes back to the ball to make himself an available target. Possess a sense of timing and eye/hand coordination and uses the sidelines well. Plays with balance as well as body control. Tracks the pass in the air, gets vertical and high-points the ball over defenders. Possesses soft as well as strong hands and consistently plucks the ball away from his frame. Gives effort blocking downfield.
column13: Plays to his 40 time, lacks the second gear and isnÂ’t a vertical threat. Displays limited quickness in his game.
column14: Since stepping in with the first team as a sophomore, Roberts has been reliable and productive for BYU, coming up with important receptions during critical moments of the game. He's a traditional possession receiver who would flourish in a timing or West Coast offense.

column12: Physical, hard-charging linebacker who gets the most from his ability. Fires upfield to defend the run, flows laterally to the sidelines and plays with reckless abandon. Breaks down well, willingly takes on blocks and uses his hands effectively. Quickly picks up coverage assignments, keeps the action in front of him and easily redirects to the play. Gets depth on pass drops, tracks the pass in the air and contorts or adjusts to defend or intercept the throw.
column13: Takes too many steps and incorrect angles and overpursues plays on occasion. Plays to his 40 time and does not display outstanding pursuit speed.
column14: Elliott is a well-built, hard-charging defender who really gets after it and works hard to make plays. He does a little bit of everything well, yet he lacks the physical skills to be an early or mid-round choice. Elliott is a Day 3 selection who should find a home as a backup linebacker/special-teams player.

column12: Michigan transfer who proved himself to be a solid downfield pass-catching threat this season. Quickly releases off the line into pass routes, tracks the ball in the air and adjusts backwards then extends to make the reception away from his frame. Displays good route discipline, works his hands to separate from defenders and battles to come away with the difficult catch. Bends his knees and stays square as a blocker and displays outstanding vision. Gives effort blocking and is effective on the second level or in motion.
column13: Possesses average blocking strength and does not finish off opponents. Moderately productive throughout his college career.
column14: Hibner was off the scouting radar prior to the season, yet a productive 2025 when he made a lot of receptions downfield has scouts intrigued. He possesses nice length and offers possibilities as a move tight end or perhaps an H-back.

column12: Productive receiver who is coming off a career season. Fluid releasing off the line of scrimmage, quickly gets in and out of routes and effectively times receptions. Tracks the pass in the air, displays eye/hand coordination and flashes strong hands with the ability to pull the ball from the air. Consistently extends to make the reception away from his frame, adjusts or contorts to the errant throw and attracts a lot of attention of double teams in the secondary. Plays faster than his 40 time and beats defenders downfield. Terrific underneath receiver who makes the reception in stride.
column13: Must do a better job coming back to the quarterback rather than waiting for the pass to get to him. Seems to give half-hearted efforts if he's not involved in the action. Loses focus and lets catchable throws slip through his hands.
column14: After a moderately productive junior campaign, McAlister watched his game take off last year, finishing the season with 72 receptions, 1,190 yards and 10 TDs. He possesses the ability necessary to be a third wideout on Sundays, but McAlister must give consistent effort and do the little things well to reach his potential.

column12: Three-year starter who began his college career with Utah. Quick, explosive and fires off the snap with a terrific first step. Plays with proper pad level, gets leverage on opponents and works his hands throughout the action. Flashes power, penetrates the line of scrimmage and forces the action. Collected a lot of double-team blocks playing over center last season. Displays agility as well as a variety of moves to protect himself. Quickly locates the ball handler, immediately changes direction and nicely redirects to the action. Makes a lot of plays with hustle and quickness. Shows pass-rush ability.
column14: Tanuvasa is a hard-working defensive lineman who does the little things well and gets the most from his ability. He must get stronger, but he offers possibilities as a backup in a four-man line.

column12: Undersized but explosive edge rusher who began his career at the University of Miami and can be used out of a three-point stance as well as standing over tackle. Quick off the snap, fluid and plays with proper pad level. Effective when asked to twist or stunt, explosive and consistently gets leverage on opponents. Quickly gets upfield and works his hands throughout the action. Bends the edge with speed, easily changes direction and quickly pursues plays from the back side. Flashes the ability to drop off the line of scrimmage and play in space. Stays with assignments and does more than pin his ears back and rush upfield.
column13: Possesses average size as well as bulk and is easily out-positioned from the action. Production was pedestrian throughout his college career
column14: Possesses a tall, thin build and gets easily locked up by blockers. Plays to his 40 time and doesn't show burst in his game. Totaled only 8.5 sacks the past two seasons.

column12: Three-year starter at left tackle who is best in a small area. Strong, effective with his hands and controls opponents at the point of attack. Sets with a wide base, shows explosiveness and quickly gets into blocks. Shows excellent awareness, keeps his head on a swivel and always looks for someone to hit. Makes proper use of angles in pass protection, stays square and rides defenders from their angles of attack. Jolts opponents with explosive hand punch and seals them from the action as a run blocker. Works hard and plays through the whistle. Gets the most from his ability.
column13: Lacks footwork and range off the edge in pass protection, struggles with speed rushers and cannot adjust to the blitz. Lacks agility, which hurts his ability to finish blocks.
column14: Adams was productive as well as durable on the college level and will have an opportunity to be a backup guard on Sundays.

column12: Three year-starter who completed 64% of his throws for more than 10,000 yards during his college career. Touch-and-timing passer who is patient in the pocket, goes through progressions and does not force the ball to covered targets. Incredibly poised and composed throughout the action, senses the rush and works hard to avoid defenders and make positive plays. Spreads the ball around to all his targets and displays a terrific sense for what's happening on the field along with pocket presence. Solid short-to-intermediate passer with a sense of timing on throws. Displays excellent field vision and takes the safe underneath outlet when nothing else is available.
column13: Lacks a next-level arm, cannot drive passes and is less accurate the further downfield he throws the ball. Gets wild when he tries to put extra speed on throws. Had a lot of passes dropped in a disappointing final campaign and totaled just 16 touchdown passes to six interceptions.
column14: Big things were expected from Klubnik last year after a terrific 2024 season, yet he fell short of the mark like many of his Clemson teammates. He's a quarterback who lacks next-level physical skills and must rely on his head to make plays as much as his arm. He offers possibilities as a backup in a timing or West Coast offense and will not fit a system that requires him to regularly throw the ball downfield.

column12: Two-year starter who began his college career at Florida. Tall, quick and surehanded. Solid route runner who quickly gets in and out of breaks, stays low on exit and extends to offer the quarterback a target. Possesses terrific eye/hand coordination, uses his frame to shield away opponents and consistently makes the catch with his hands. Tracks the pass in the air, keeps the play in bounds after the reception and battles opponents to come away with the contested grab.
column13: Plays to one speed and isnÂ’t a vertical threat. Must improve his route running.
column14: Douglas is a surehanded wideout who would be effective in a timing or West Coast offense and projects as a No. 4 wideout on Sundays.

column12: Large, efficient tight end who is effective as a pass catcher and as a blocker. Strong, gets leverage on defenders and blocks with a nasty attitude. Starts with excellent knee bend, quickly gets to the second level and gives effort in motion. Chips defenders on his way into routes to disrupt their angles of attack and seals opponents from the action. Quickly releases into pass routes and stays low exiting breaks. Extends his hands and works to make the reception away from his frame. Gets vertical and adjusts or contorts to come away with the difficult grab. Sturdy, offers the quarterback a large target and takes a pounding yet holds on to the ball.
column13: Lacks a burst and plays to his 40 time. More of a short-range target. Watched his production drop from 50 receptions in 2024 to just 29 last season.
column14: Royer is an efficient tight end who does well in every aspect of the position, yet he lacks great speed as well as overwhelming power. He possesses enough skill to line up as a No. 2 tight end on Sundays who will be brought onto the field for short-yardage or goal-line situations to be used as a blocker or pass catcher.

column12: Large, powerful and somewhat athletic defensive tackle who takes up a lot of room in the middle of the line. Shows power in his lower body and knocks blockers back off the ball. Plays with leverage, easily changes direction and has the ability to wreck opponents' gameplans. Quickly gets his hands up and uses them to protect himself. Easily discards blockers then redirects to make plays against the run. Controls opponents or slides off them altogether to get to the action. Flashes an explosive first step, keeps his feet moving and moves well in any direction.
column13: Very streaky and failed to live up to expectations the past two seasons. Marginal pass rusher who totaled just three sacks during his six-year career at Clemson. Knocked from the action by lesser opponents at times.
column14: Capehart was highly regarded in the scouting community prior to the 2024 season, yet he chose to go back to Clemson after a disappointing campaign. He was hit-and-miss throughout last season and at present is more bark than bite. He possesses an upside and could be a dominant force on the inside of a four-man line, yet he must start to play to his level of ability on every snap.

column12: Underrated linebacker prospect who is coming off a terrific senior season. Immediately reads or diagnoses plays, remains disciplined with assignments and stays with the action. Breaks down well, effectively uses his hands and quickly locates the ball handler. Efficient, takes proper angles to the action and discards blockers to get to the ball. Constantly around the action, effective upfield or outside the box and works hard to make plays. Turned in three terrific days of practice at the Shrine Bowl and looked like the most complete linebacker on the field.
column13: Possesses a thin build and is easily out-positioned from the action. Marginally quick and does not show great top-end speed. Stiff as well as methodical moving in reverse on passing downs.
column14: Dugger was a tremendous run-stuffing linebacker last season who made a lot of plays behind the line of scrimmage. Finishing the year with 118 tackles, 11 tackles for loss as well as three sacks, he is a disruptive force, but he must fill out his frame. He has enough ability to get consideration at inside or middle linebacker and plays with a special-teams mentality.

column12: Productive pass rusher who gets the most from his ability. Quick off the snap, plays with terrific pad level and works his hands throughout the action. Incredibly quick, moves well around the field and possesses good change-of-direction skills with the ability to immediately alter his angle of attack. Agile, rarely off his feet and displays a nice inside move. Resilient, chases the action hard and plays with consistent leverage. Was one of the best pass rushers during Shrine Bowl practice.
column13: Easily disrupted by the initial block or out-positioned from the action. Not a great run defender.
column14: Williams is a hard-working lineman who made a lot of plays behind the line of scrimmage the past two seasons and projects as a situational/rotational pass rusher on Sundays who can be used in a variety of schemes.

column12: Athletic defensive tackle with next-level size/speed numbers. Starts with excellent knee bend, gets leverage on opponents and quickly gets his hands up to protect himself. Command double-team blocks in the middle of the line, keeps his feet moving and displays good change-of-direction skills. Resilient, plays off blocks and works to get to the action.
column13: Does not play like an overpowering 320-pound defensive tackle. Doesn't show the ability to get down the line of scrimmage and make plays outside the box despite his 40 time. Possesses short arms. Marginal production on the college level.
column14: At face value, Cleveland possesses the size, speed and computer numbers to be a middle-round pick, yet the film says otherwise. He's a marginal pass rusher who occasionally makes plays against the run, and he must start to meet expectations to have a career in the NFL.

column12: Large right tackle prospect who began his college career at Washington State. Quickly sets up in pass protection, immediately gets into run blocks and plays with strength. Fires his hands into defenders, blocks with proper lean and stays square. Patient, keeps his head on a swivel and controls opponents once engaged at the point. Blocks with a nasty attitude, drives defenders off the line and works to annihilate them. Flashes ability in pass protection, keeps his feet moving and effectively uses his hands.
column13: Lacks balance blocking in motion. Inconsistent in pass protection, doesn't make good use of angles and gets knocked back into the pocket by lesser opponents.
column14: Fa'amoe was a four-year starter on the college level and a consistent lineman who gave great effort. He has limitations, but he has the ability to be effective as a backup right tackle.

column12: Once-highly rated receiver who returned from legal woes last year to have a productive season. Quickly releases off the line into pass routes, is sharp in and out of breaks and stays low on exit. Tracks the pass in the air, extends his hands and snatches the ball away from his frame. Offers the quarterback a target and possesses soft, quick hands. Gets vertical and adjusts or contorts to the errant throw and comes away with the difficult reception even when defenders are draped on him. Possesses a strong build and out-muscles opponents to make the catch. Shows focus as well as concentration, possesses eye/hand coordination and consistently comes away with the difficult grab.
column13: Plays to one speed and isn't a true downfield threat. Did not have great pass-catching production during his college career. Dismissed from the Georgia program in 2024 after being charged with child cruelty and family violence, charges he ultimately reached a plea deal on and received probation for.
column14: Thomas is a skilled pass catcher who has shown next-level ability throughout his college career and did a terrific job at Troy last season after being suspended the prior year. He possesses an NFL build, reliable hands and the ability to line up in a timing offense on Sundays.

column12: Three-year starter who has been a dominant run defender on the college level. Physical, forceful and competes throughout the action. Fires upfield to defend the run, take proper angles and wraps up tackling. Quickly picks up coverage assignments, displays skill over the slot receiver and nicely tracks the ball in the air. Fights through blocks to get to the ball handler and make the play.
column13: Lacks an explosive closing burst or break to the ball out of his plant. Lacks speed to the sidelines and is better facing the action in coverage.
column14: Payne was a forceful yet intelligent safety at Kansas State who is best between the numbers. He comes with outstanding size, yet he may be limited to a two-deep zone scheme, and Payne must make his mark on special teams.

column12: Productive college quarterback with marginal upside for the next level. Possesses a quick release and a live arm and gets the ball through the tight spots. Natural looking off the safety, goes through progressions and takes the underneath outlet when nothing else is available. Patient, spreads the ball around and knows where receivers are on the field. Displays a sense of timing on throws and does not make receivers wait on the ball. Relatively accurate. Show some elusiveness and the ability to pick up yardage with his legs.
column13: Seemed to struggle last year, made poor decisions under the rush and held the ball too long. Unnecessarily releases passes off his back foot. Not a big quarterback and cannot withstand the rush.
column14: Morton was a terrific college quarterback who possesses a decent but not elite arm and lacks next-level size. He could back up in offense that does not employ the vertical game, though he'll never be a consistent starter on Sundays.

column12: Massive safety who rebounded after missing 2024 with injury to have an outstanding campaign. Outstanding run defender who crashes upfield, flies around the football and works hard to make plays. Explosive hitter who is a solid open-field tackler. Diagnoses plays, tracks the ball in the air and has a nice move to the throw in coverage.
column13: Does not show an explosive closing burst to the ball out of his plant. Possesses poor hands for the interception. Lacks great range.
column14: After a tremendous campaign with South Dakota State in 2023 when he intercepted eight passes and recorded 93 tackles, Wisniewski sat on the sidelines in 2024 with injury. He returned and played incredibly well at Texas Tech last season then looked outstanding during Shrine Bowl practices. He's a traditional strong safety with potential in a two-deep system who could surprise on Sundays if he continues to improve his game.

column12: Nice-sized cornerback who has flashed ability the past two seasons. Effective facing the action, communicates well with teammates in the secondary and quickly reads and diagnoses plays. Physical, battles receivers throughout the route and stays with assignments. Nicely times pass defenses and jumps the route to make plays. Possesses good hands for the interception.
column13: Slow to react to receivers' moves off the line of scrimmage and gives up inside leverage. Does a lot of face-guarding in man coverage. Lacks an explosive closing burst.
column14: Wright is athletic cornerback with decent size who has flashed ability the past two seasons. He comes with a large upside, but he must really polish his game and would be best facing the action in a zone scheme early in his NFL career.

column12: Athletic tight end prospect with a large upside. Smooth, fluid and easily moves about the field. Settles into the open spot, extends his hands to offer the quarterback a target and makes the reception away from his frame. Often lines up in the slot, quickly releases off the line of scrimmage and easily adjusts to the errant throw. Consistent hands catcher who sells routes and plays like a good athlete. Tracks the pass in the air and gets vertical or contorts to come away with the reception. Blocks with leverage, squares into defenders and seals them from the action.
column13: Plays to his 40 time and does not show elite downfield speed. Doesn't block with a killer mentality. Prone to dropping passes on occasion. Marginally productive and posted a career-best 34 receptions for 319 receiving yards last year.
column14: Rogers is a tight end prospect who has shown ability as well as development in his game and comes with an upside. He's a move tight end prospect with the potential to start off as a No. 3 at the position then improve from that point.

column12: Penn State transfer who showed consistent improvement in his game the past two seasons. Explodes off the snap with a terrific first step, fights with his hands and works hard to get through blocks. Resilient. Moves well in every direction. Plays with leverage, gets a lot of momentum going upfield and looks as though he was shot out of a cannon at times. Consistently penetrates the line of scrimmage and was credited with 11 TFLs last season.
column13: Lacks natural bulk and strength and gets engulfed at the point or taken from the action by a single blocker. Never proved himself to be a top pass rusher in college.
column14: van den Berg was a defensive lineman who consistently improved on the college level and projects as a three-technique or one-gap lineman in a four-man front.

column12: Productive three-year starter who is reliable, intelligent and works hard. Finds the open spot in the defense, extends his hands to offer the quarterback a target and consistently makes the reception away from his frame. Tough, physical receiver despite his slight build. Battles and works to come away with the catch. Tracks the deep pass in the air, looks the ball into his hands and easily makes the reception at full speed. Has a burst off the line that enables him to beat defenders, effectively times receptions and shows run-after-the-catch ability. Adjusts to errant throws or gets down to scoop up low passes. Effective on reverses.
column13: Small with a slight build and quarterbacks will struggle to find him unless he's wide open. Has small hands and short arms. Had his senior season interrupted by injury.
column14: Rutherford is an explosive athlete who plays bigger than his listed size, besides being a terrific receiver. Size is a limiting factor, yet his ability as a pass catcher, reverse runner and return specialist will give him a chance to make an NFL roster as a fifth receiver.

column12: Tall, productive pass rusher who can stand over tackle or come out of a three-point stance. Agile, flattens off the edge and pursues from the back side. Easily changes direction, displays an inside move and has a variety of techniques with his hands to protect himself or get off blocks. Stays with assignments, possesses excellent movement skills and shows enough athleticism to be used in space. Resilient, keeps his feet moving and plays faster than his 40 time.
column13: Lacks bulk and is easily out-positioned from the action. Does not show a great closing burst.
column14: Anthony was a terrific defender for Duke the past two seasons, totaling 22.5 TFLs and 12.5 sacks. He must get bigger and stronger, yet he possesses an upside and could be a situational pass rusher early in his NFL career.

column12: Aggressive cornerback who flashes ball skills. Quick flipping his hips in transition, physical and mixes it up with receivers throughout the route. Smooth pedaling in reverse, works to get his head back around to track the pass and nicely times pass defenses. Effectively diagnoses plays, shows agility and has an explosive burst to the ball out of his plant.
column13: Deep speed is a concern. Sometimes struggles to find the ball in the air. Dealt with injury last season and missed time.
column14: Harvey flashed ability the past three years at Georgia Tech, totaling 63 tackles as a junior then breaking up seven passes last season. He possesses solid size and offers potential in both zone or man coverage, but he must finish his game to make an NFL roster.

column12: Slightly undersized pass-catching tight end who quickly releases off the line of scrimmage into routes, plays like a good athlete and uses his hands to separate from defenders. Nicely makes the catch in stride, adjusts backwards to the pass and looks the ball into his hands. Extends his hands to offer the quarterback a target, tracks the pass in the air and makes the reception away from his frame. Flashes soft hands. Blocks with leverage, keeps his feet moving in pass protection and works blocks.
column13: Indecisive and has marginal blocking vision. Not a strong blocker. Inconsistent, drops catchable throws and does not always come away with the difficult catch.
column14: Franklin was a productive pass catcher at Boston College the past two seasons, yet he has gaps in his game, which is concerning for the next level. He's built like a move tight end, yet he has been inconsistent catching the ball and average as a blocker. Franklin comes with upside and is worth selecting in the late rounds.

column12: Large, strong, power gap prospect who was a four-year starter. Stays square and blocks with explosiveness and excellent lean. Shows excellent recognition and controls defenders once engaged at the point. Effective when he properly places his hands into opponents. Mostly blocks with proper fundamentals, turns defenders off the line and opens up running lanes. Keeps his head on a swivel and works well with linemates.
column13: Stiff and slow adjusting to inside moves. Lack of agility hurts his ability to finish blocks. Lacks footwork and ability in space.
column14: Taylor is an accomplished straight-ahead, power-blocking linemen who is best in a small area. He's a late-round prospect who could eventually break into the starting lineup two years down the road.

column12: Two-year starter at left tackle who projects to guard in the NFL. Quick off the snap, stays square and seals defenders from the action. Fires out to the second level, displays blocking vision and plays with leverage. Quick, explosive and fluid pulling across the line of scrimmage to block in motion. Fights hard and plays with a nasty attitude.
column13: Tends to fall off defenders and rarely finishes blocks. Marginal strength and does not get much movement run blocking.
column14: Mosesso is a hard-working college lineman who displayed limited progress in his game last season after a promising junior campaign. He lacks the athleticism for a zone blocking system and the strength to be used in a power gap scheme, which will make it an uphill battle at the next level.

column12: Undersized, athletic run-and-chase linebacker who is quick, fluid and displays excellent speed as well as movement skills in every direction. Gets depth on pass drops, possesses an explosive closing burst of speed and wraps up tackling. Quickly locates the ball handler, easily redirects to the action and immediately alters his angle of attack. Fires upfield to defend the run and gives effort on the blitz. Displays a lot of ability in pursuit and quickly gets to the sidelines. Quarterbacks the defense and makes the calls for the unit.
column13: Was rotated in and out of the lineup during a slightly disappointing 2025 season. Seemed to do a lot of running around rather than playmaking in 2025. Has just two interceptions in his college career despite his athleticism.
column14: Bissainthe is an undersized one-gap linebacker who can be used on the inside of the 3-4 as a traditional weakside defender, but he must start to show improvement in his game and build upon what was a terrific junior campaign in 2024.

column12: Two-year starter who has been very productive. Quick flipping his hips in transition, battles opponents throughout the route and works to get his head back around to track the pass. Keeps opponents in front of him, nicely times pass defenses and works hard to get involved and make the play. Displays good route recognition facing the action. Gives a lot of effort defending the run. Effective special-teams player who helps on coverage units.
column13: Lacks top-end speed and does a bit of trailing downfield. Must improve his footwork and skills moving in reverse. Possesses small hands and measures under six feet tall.
