column14: Smith was a consistent cornerback at Toledo who totaled 100 tackles and was credited with 28 pass defenses the past two seasons, when he also blocked a pair of kicks. Best facing the action, he offers possibilities as a dime back in zone coverage and can also line up on coverage units.

column12: Nice-sized ball carrier who has also been a sensational pass catcher out of the backfield the past two seasons. Quick-footed, bounces around piles and defenders and falls forward when tackled. Elusive, creates yardage and possesses terrific field vision. Displays eye/hand coordination as a receiver, possesses soft as well as strong hands and makes a lot of difficult receptions. Quickly gets into pass routes, tracks the pass in the air and shows great focus as well as concentration. Pass-catching totals last year included 51 receptions, 941 yards and 16 TDs after 39 receptions for 671 yards the prior year.
column13: Never rushed for more than 500 yards in a single season. Lacks the speed and agility necessary to turn the corner. Loses momentum trying to immediately alter his angle of attack or cut back against the grain.
column14: Heidenreich was a tremendous pass-catching threat for a Navy offense that preferred to run the ball most of the time. He possesses the size as well as skill to line up as a rotational or third down back once he opts for the NFL.

column12: Underrated blocker who has been a full-time starter since his freshman season. Fundamentally sound, quickly sets up in pass protection and explodes into run blocks. Agile, stays square and turns defenders from the action. Immediately gets his hands into opponents, blocks with a nasty attitude and fluidly pulls across the line of scrimmage. Controls opponents once engaged the point and is large enough to engulf them altogether.
column13: Struggles to adjust and gets beaten by quick or nimble opponents. May lack the pure athleticism to be used in a zone-blocking system.
column14: Mokofisi was a terrific four-year interior offensive lineman for Utah and shows dominance as a run blocker while holding his own in pass protection. He does not get the credit he deserves in the scouting community and will be a great find late in the draft or as a priority free agent who makes a roster as a backup lineman.

column12: Three-year starter who led TCU in tackles as a sophomore and junior. Stays with assignments, works hard to get involved in the action and sells out to make plays. Breaks down well, uses his hands to protect himself and flows well laterally. Works to defeat blocks and set the edge and allows teammates to make plays on the ball. Nicely redirects or adjusts to make the tackle. Displays solid range, nicely adjusts or redirects to ball handlers and wraps up tackling.
column13: Doesn't play to his 40 time or show great speed in pursuit. Lack of size is a red flag.
column14: Obiazor as a terrific college linebacker who totaled 80 tackles or more each of the past three seasons. He needs to further develop his game, yet he comes with an upside and has special-teams potential.

column12: Hard-working tight end who gets the most from his ability. Quickly releases off the line of scrimmage into pass routes, uses his hands to separate from defenders and extends to make the reception away from his frame. Sturdy receiver who adjusts backwards for the errant throw and wins out in battles. Keeps the play in bounds after the catch and works to pick up positive yardage. Displays blocking vision, quickly gets to the second level and squares into linebackers to take them from the action. Shows ability as a position blocker and gives effort.
column13: Does not play like a top athlete and isn't much faster than his 40 time. Lets too many catchable passes slip away. Possesses average blocking strength and struggles finishing off linebackers.
column14: Roush was a productive tight end for Stanford the past two seasons and possesses a next-level frame, yet he lacks the athleticism and natural pass-catching hands to be anything other than a third tight end on Sundays.

column12: Two-year starter who began his college career at Louisville. Powerful and works to bend his knees and block with leverage. Anchors in pass protection and turns defenders from the line run blocking. Works incredibly well with linemates, competes and keeps his head on a swivel. Jolts defenders with explosive hand punch and knocks them from the action while also displaying the ability to kick out or pull across the line of scrimmage. Effective position blocker who stays square and works through the whistle.
column13: Struggles with balance the larger the area he is asked to cover. Lacks footwork in space. Possesses arms under 33 inches.
column14: Baynes is a strong and somewhat athletic interior offensive lineman who can make an NFL roster as a backup for a variety of blocking schemes.

column12: Quick and explosive one-gap defensive tackle who can line up at the three-technique spot. Plays with terrific pad level, gets leverage on opponents and shows mobility. Gives great effort, easily redirects to the action and works to make plays against the run. Effectively uses his hands, keeps his feet moving and flashes power in his game. Smooth moving laterally, fights with his hands and stays with the action.
column13: Lacks bulk and strength and gets tied up in blocks or out-positioned from the action. Does not display a great first step for a smaller lineman. Average production the past two seasons and does not present himself to be a great pass rusher.
column14: Marshall is a hard-charging lineman who struggled playing end in Baylor's three-man line last season. He has enough skill to be a backup in a four-man front on Sundays.

column12: Athletic and quick defensive back who is coming off a sensational senior season. Effectively reads or diagnoses the action, quickly picks up coverage assignments and moves well laterally. Runs sideline to sideline, tracks the pass in the air and displays solid ball skills. Shows ability in man coverage and works well with cornerbacks. Works to keep the action in front of him, explodes upfield and sells out to defend the run. Senior totals included 104 tackles, five interceptions and three PBUs
column13: Lacks a quick and fluid backpedal. Show stiffness in his game. Better making plays up the field than in reverse.
column14: Moses entered the season graded as a potential late-round pick, and his senior campaign could cement him in that area of the draft. He lacks great size, but he shows a head for the football and a disciplined game.

column12: Productive running back who is coming off a somewhat disappointing season. Patient ball carrier with outstanding vision and instincts. Follows blocks everywhere on the field, finds the open lanes and quickly cuts back against the grain without losing momentum. Quick-footed, possesses an effective jump cut and makes defenders miss. Possesses the agility necessary to turn the corner. Works hard with blocking assignments. Solid pass catcher out of the backfield.
column13: Not a strong back who moves the pile or picks up much yardage off contact. Easily brought down in the open field by a single defender. Was used in a rotational role last season. Plays to one speed.
column14: Henderson rushed for 1,078 yards and nine TDs as a junior, which resulted in scouts giving him a Day 3 grade before the season, but he struggled in 2025. Finishing the year with 755 yards and six TDs, he did a solid job sharing the ball, which is the way he'll have to make a roster on Sundays.

column12: Massive and powerful interior offensive lineman with an underrated game. Bends his knees, blocks with leverage and quickly gets his hands into defenders. Stays square, keeps his feet moving and anchors at the point in pass protection. Powerful and turns defenders off the line to open running lanes. Stays with assignments and keeps his head on a swivel.
column13: Stiff. Heavy on his feet and struggles to pull across the line of scrimmage.
column14: Cooper comes with an NFL build, long arms and the playing style to line up in a power gap scheme. While he’s just a small-area blocker, he would excel in an offense that prefers to run the ball.

column12: Florida International transfer who has been very productive the past two seasons. Tall, built well and plays strong football. Engages receivers at the line of scrimmage, quickly picks up assignments and mixes it up throughout the route. Instinctive, shows good route recognition and tracks the pass in the air. Possesses a nice move to the throw, effectively times pass defenses and out-muscles opponents to knock away passes. Tracks the ball in the air and works well with teammates. Fires upfield to defend screen throws. Hard-hitting and wraps up tackling.
column13: Must improve his overall footwork, as he loses opponents changing direction. Very quick out of his backpedal and prefers to side shuffle. Slow getting his head back around and better facing the action.
column14: Masses was a solid cornerback the past four years, but he really stood out after transferring to Cal last season, when he led the nation with18 pass defenses. He possesses next-level size and would be a nice addition for a zone system.

column12: Well-traveled receiver who is coming off a career season. Settles into the open spot of the defense, extends his hands to offer the quarterback a target and consistently makes the reception away from his frame. Tracks the pass in the air, adjusts to the throw and nicely makes the reception in stride. Uses the sidelines well and displays a sense of timing as well as eye/hand coordination and focus. Makes the difficult over-the-shoulder catch in contorted positions.
column13: Unnecessarily leaves his feet on occasion. Has a limited route tree. Was marginally productive until last season.
column14: After pedestrian numbers at BYU the prior two years, Marion finished the 2025 campaign with 56 receptions, 740 yards and two TDs. He's an athletic wideout with a solid build and next-level speed. The development he displayed last season should push him into the late rounds.

column12: USC transfer who flashed ability the past two seasons. Patient in the pocket, buys time for receivers and takes a big hit to get the throw away. Senses the rush, steps up to avoid defenders and takes off upfield to pick up yardage with his legs. Does a terrific job selling ball fakes, puts touch on passes and delivers a catchable ball. Keeps his eyes downfield while escaping the rush, shows the ability to layer the ball between defenders and remains poised throughout the action. Puts good speed on short and intermediate passes. Only leaves the pocket when necessary, keeps his eyes downfield and effectively commands the offense.
column13: Possesses an average deep arm. Not a big or sturdy pocket passer and easily gets brought down under the rush. Tends to stare down the primary target and is slow to find defenders in the secondary.
column14: Moss was a consistent passer the prior two seasons and has enough ability to get consideration as a third quarterback.
